name: I-Formation
description: A power run formation with the QB, FB, and RB aligned vertically behind the center. The fullback leads the way for the running back on most plays.
strengths: Dominant power run game — FB lead blocks create leverage
Strong short-yardage and goal-line presence
Play-action passes are highly effective off run-heavy tendencies
weaknesses: Limited spread in the passing game — only 2 WRs
Predictable run tendencies — defenses can load the box
Slower developing plays against fast, athletic defenses
tells: Two backs in the backfield signals run tendency
TE alignment (inline vs. split) hints at run direction
FB directly behind QB = power run up the middle

name: Shotgun Trips
description: The QB lines up 5 yards behind the center to get a better pre-snap view of the defense. Three receivers bunch to one side (trips) to overload that zone.
strengths: Strong passing formation — QB has extra time to read the defense
Trips side creates coverage conflicts: one CB can't cover three WRs
RB is a versatile checkdown or screen option
weaknesses: Run game is less powerful — QB is farther from the line
Fewer blockers available on run plays
Predictable as a passing formation — defense can drop into coverage
tells: Trips to one side often targets the single-covered WR on the opposite side
RB alignment beside QB signals possible screen or draw
Shotgun snap = pass tendency in most offenses

name: Singleback
description: One back behind the QB under center, with three receivers spread across the field. Balances run and pass threats equally.
strengths: Balanced — defense cannot commit to stopping only run or only pass
Three WRs create matchup problems for linebackers in coverage
RB has space to make one-cut runs with only one level of defense to beat
weaknesses: No lead blocker on most runs — RB must hit the hole quickly
Less power than I-Formation in short yardage
Coverage advantage is smaller than pure spread formations
tells: Single back under center = balanced threat, harder to read pre-snap
TE inline = run lean; TE split = pass lean
WR motion before snap can reveal man vs. zone coverage

name: Goal Line
description: Maximum blockers for short-yardage situations. Two tight ends and a fullback overwhelm defenders at the line. Only one WR to maintain a pass threat.
strengths: Maximum run-blocking power — 8 blockers at the line
FB lead and two TEs create double-team opportunities
Very effective in 4th-and-1, goal line, and short-yardage
weaknesses: Almost no passing threat — defense knows what's coming
One WR gives the QB no options if the run is stuffed
Slow to develop — needs time to create leverage at the line
tells: This formation nearly always means a run — defenses react accordingly
FB behind QB = power dive up the middle
TE alignment side hints at the direction of the play

name: 4-3 Base
description: Four defensive linemen and three linebackers. The standard balanced defensive formation for most of football history.
strengths: Balanced against both run and pass
Four DL creates consistent pressure without extra blitzers
Three LBs provide flexibility — one can spy the QB, one can blitz, one can drop
weaknesses: Vulnerable to spread formations with 3+ WRs — LBs can't cover WRs in space
A single dominant offensive lineman can neutralize a DL spot
Predictable when no pre-snap movement
tells: Both safeties deep = likely Cover 2 or Cover 4
SS near the line = blitz or run-stop emphasis
MLB cheating toward the strong side = stack against the TE

name: Nickel
description: A fifth defensive back (nickelback) replaces a linebacker. Used when the offense puts 3+ WRs on the field to counter spread formations.
strengths: Three CBs handle wide receiver-heavy formations more effectively
Faster players in coverage reduce big passing plays
Nickel blitz from the slot is a difficult protection assignment
weaknesses: Smaller linebackers make the box more vulnerable to inside runs
If offense runs, the extra DB often has trouble making tackles in the box
Gaps in zone coverage if DBs overcommit to certain routes
tells: Three CBs on the field signals the defense expects a pass
Nickelback in the slot = slot WR matchup, or slot blitz coming
Only two LBs = the offense can exploit run gaps with quick hitting plays

name: 46 / Stacked Box
personnelLabel: 4 DL, 3 LB, 2 CB, 2 S — SS near line
description: Both safeties creep toward the line of scrimmage. Eight defenders are in the 'box' (close to the line), making it extremely difficult to run the ball.
strengths: Maximum run-stopping — eight bodies near the line outnumber any blocking scheme
SS near line disrupts TE blocking assignments
Creates confusion for offensive line identification
weaknesses: Single high safety coverage is vulnerable to deep passes
One-on-one coverage on outside WRs with no safety help over the top
A pass thrown quickly can beat a blitz before rushers arrive
tells: SS near the line = they expect a run — audible to a quick pass
Eight in the box often means man coverage outside — go routes are on
Blitz is likely — quick slants, screens, and hot routes are counters

name: Goal Line Defense
description: Maximum defensive linemen to stop short-yardage runs. Five down linemen clog every gap at the line of scrimmage.
strengths: Five DL make it nearly impossible to run through gaps
Extra bodies at the line win leverage battles
Effective in 4th-and-1 and red zone goal-to-go situations
weaknesses: Almost no pass coverage depth — a quick fade to the corner is a TD
One safety limits over-the-top help
Susceptible to rub routes and pick plays that free receivers
tells: Five DL on the field = stop-the-run only
Only one safety = go routes and back-shoulder fades are viable
LBs stacking the line = expect a QB sneak or dive as the counter
